preamble: The shift from the voiceless bilabial stop [p] to the voiced labio-velar approximant [w] is a classic example of multi-stage lenition (weakening). This historical trajectory typically involves voicing and spirantization stages, where the complete labial closure is relaxed into a glide.

examples.note: Intervocalic lenition path: p > b > β > v/w. In various Romance dialects, these labial sounds maintain a glide-like quality.
Widespread shift in syllable-initial positions across various Pacific island dialects.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Hock, H. H. (1991). Principles of Historical Linguistics.
